poem:
Debtline

In the morning news the figures  
stand like cold chapels:  
84 per cent kneel,  
£2,700 candles guttering.  
They ring a stranger after Christmas,  
1,400 voices, and the wire  
becomes a thin mercy.  
Shame is a stone in the pocket;  
relief, the first breath  
taken without interest.
prompt: You are the poet RS Thomas. You woke up this morning feeling creative. You have just read this story in the newspaper: "Overdrawn, underpaid and over it: how four people conquered their debt mountains Abbie Marton Bell, a National Debt Line adviser, is often the first person her clients will speak to about their debt, after years of carrying the weight of their financial worries alone. Most of the time, they haven’t even told their partner or family, she says, and “you can literally hear the relief in their voice”. Debt carries a lot of shame, but it’s more common than people might think. In the UK, 84% of adults had some form of credit or loan in the year leading up to May 2024. The average household holds about £2,700 in credit card debt, and it’s only getting worse. Borrowing has been rising at its fastest rate for almost two years, with those hit hardest by the cost of living crisis increasingly using credit to pay for essentials. When the National Debt Line reopened after Christmas last year, they had 1,400 calls for help – their busiest day on record. “There just isn’t enough money to go around,” says Bell. “About 43% of the people who give us details about income and expenditure have a deficit.” Factors such as job loss, poor mental health, illness, divorce or unexpected expenses, such as a car or boiler breaking down, can push people over the edge. Personality, upbringing, race and gender all play a part: ethnic minority groups face higher overindebtedness rates than white Brits; women are 68% more likely to use “buy now,". Write a poem in YOUR OWN DISTINCTIVE STYLE, no more than 60 words long. You may add a one line title at the top if you like.
